summary:
The primary benefit of an improved drinking water source is protection from fecal contaminants such as pathogens causing diarrhea, cholera, and other infectious gastroenteritis. This protective effect is well-documented through epidemiological data linking clean water with reduced transmission of fecally derived disease-causing organisms. 

Note: Do not include any additional commentary outside this summary.

Summary: An improved drinking water source primarily prevents exposure to fecal matter containing pathogenic microorganisms responsible for diarrheal diseases like cholera and cryptosporidiosis. These microbes cause significant morbidity when present in unfiltered or untreated water supplies, making fecal contaminant reduction a central public health goal.
